title: Modify Strings
content:
### Modifying Strings

Strings are **immutable** -- you cannot change them directly. But you can create **modified copies**.

---

### Upper and Lower Case

```python
text = "Hello, World!"
print(text.upper())  # "HELLO, WORLD!"
print(text.lower())  # "hello, world!"
print(text.title())  # "Hello, World!"
print(text.capitalize())  # "Hello, world!"
```

---

### Removing Whitespace

```python
text = "  Hello, World!  "
print(text.strip())       # "Hello, World!" (both sides)
print(text.lstrip())      # "Hello, World!  " (left only)
print(text.rstrip())      # "  Hello, World!" (right only)
```

---

### Replace and Split

```python
text = "Hello, World!"
print(text.replace("World", "Python"))  # "Hello, Python!"
print(text.split(","))  # ["Hello", " World!"]
```

---

### Joining Strings

```python
words = ["Hello", "World"]
print(" ".join(words))   # "Hello World"
print(", ".join(words))  # "Hello, World"
```

---

### Checking String Content

```python
text = "Hello123"
print(text.isalpha())    # False (contains digits)
print(text.isdigit())    # False (contains letters)
print("123".isdigit())   # True
print("Hello".isalpha()) # True
```

---

> **Remember:** Strings are immutable. Methods like `.upper()` return a **new** string -- they don't change the original.
codeExample:
text = "  Hello, World!  "

print(text.strip())
print(text.upper())
print(text.replace("World", "Python"))

words = text.strip().split(", ")
print(" ".join(words))

print("123".isdigit())
print("Hello".isalpha())
